component_identification.content: 181 cards (8 menu categories and desserts), 22 menu tiles, 8 pawns/chopsticks markers, 1 game board, and scoring tracker. Card counts vary by menu item; board shows which sets to include each game.

core_game_loop.content: Players simultaneously choose one card from hand, reveal, then pass remaining hand left (or right by round depending variant). Cards score by set-building patterns and menu effects. After hand depletion, round scores most items; desserts often score at game end. New hands are dealt for next rounds based on player count.

detailed_rules.content: Before play, build menu by selecting item categories per board instructions (e.g., nigiri, rolls, appetizers, specials, desserts). Deal hands each round (cards per player scale by player count). Everyone picks one card and places face-up; optional chopsticks effect allows taking two cards in one turn if you played chopsticks previously, then return chopsticks card to hand. Scoring depends on included cards: Nigiri values, Wasabi triples next nigiri, Tempura pairs, Sashimi triples, Dumpling escalating sets, Maki majority/minority by icons, etc. Unused/odd cards in incomplete sets usually score zero unless card text says otherwise. After each round, clear non-dessert cards; keep desserts in front (pudding, fruit depending mode) for endgame scoring. Pass direction may alternate to reduce first-seat bias in some variants.

scoring_and_endgame.content: Default game is 3 rounds. Score most cards at round end according to active menu tile rules; track cumulative points. Desserts score at final endgame (e.g., Pudding: most gains points, least loses in 3+ players; Fruit uses mixed set chart). Highest total after dessert scoring wins, tie broken by dessert points then shared victory.

beginner_strategy.content: Learn menu interactions before drafting—Wasabi is weak unless nigiri density is high, while dumplings want steady accumulation. Early in each hand, prioritize flexible cards that keep multiple scoring lines open. Watch pass direction and count remaining cards to predict whether a needed combo piece can wheel back. In high-player games, deny majority-sensitive maki when an opponent is close to first. Don’t overcommit to one fragile combo if menu has many denial opportunities.
